1.title: Last Mile Advantage
1.page_title: (15) The Last Mile Advantage in Healthcare | LinkedIn
1.text:
The Healthcare Strategist
5,675 subscribers
Subscribed
The Last Mile Advantage in Healthcare
Mohanned Afeef
Group CSO, Saudi German Health | The Healthcare Strategist | Growth & Transformation in Health Systems
January 1, 2026

Most healthcare strategy discussions begin with scale.

How many beds we operate. How many facilities we own. How much technology we deploy. How large our clinical workforce has become.

These conversations are not wrong. But they are incomplete.

Because in healthcare, competitive advantage does not ultimately reside in what a system owns or even how efficiently it produces care. It resides in how care is experienced when the system meets the patient.

That moment happens in the last mile.

And the last mile is where most healthcare strategies quietly fail.

Reframing Advantage Through Flow

Healthcare systems can be understood through a simple but powerful lens: flow.

Flow is not speed. Flow is not volume. Flow is not utilization.

Flow is the system’s ability to move people, information, and decisions across the care continuum with minimal friction and minimal uncertainty.

When flow is fragmented, advantage erodes even in systems that appear strong on paper. When flow is orchestrated, advantage compounds even without dramatic structural expansion.

This distinction explains why some healthcare organizations feel increasingly brittle as they grow, while others become more resilient, trusted, and difficult to displace.

The Three Miles of Healthcare Value Creation

Every healthcare system operates across three interconnected layers, each corresponding to a different form of advantage.

The First Mile: Infrastructure of Care

The first mile is where healthcare organizations concentrate most of their capital and strategic attention.

Hospitals. Clinics. Diagnostic assets. Technology platforms. Clinical staffing.

This layer creates structural advantage. It defines capacity and signals seriousness. It is also highly visible, which makes it attractive to boards, investors, and regulators.

But structural advantage is inherently unstable.

Infrastructure creates potential. It does not create experience. Without coordinated flow, infrastructure becomes static, expensive, and increasingly misaligned with how care is actually consumed.

The Second Mile: Production of Care

The second mile is where healthcare organizations focus on operational performance.

Clinical protocols. Throughput. Scheduling efficiency. Length of stay. Capacity utilization.

This layer determines whether care can be delivered safely, consistently, and at scale. It is where operational excellence is earned.

Yet even here, advantage remains internal.

A system can be clinically excellent and operationally efficient while still failing its patients at the point of interaction. That is because operational performance does not automatically translate into confidence or trust.

Those are built elsewhere.

The Last Mile: Exposure of Care

The last mile is the only part of the system the patient truly experiences.

Appointments. Handoffs. Waiting. Results. Billing. Follow-up. Uncertainty.

This is where abstract strategy becomes tangible reality.

And it is where advantage is either revealed or invalidated.

Patients do not evaluate healthcare systems based on balance sheets or operating margins. They evaluate them based on predictability, clarity, and continuity. The last mile is where these attributes are either present or absent.

Why Structural Advantage Quietly Decays

Most healthcare systems begin from a position of structural strength.

They invest heavily in facilities, talent, and technology. Early on, demand follows supply. Growth appears justified.

Then friction accumulates.

Transitions break down. Information does not move cleanly. Ownership becomes unclear. Patients repeat their story. Results arrive late or without context.

None of this looks catastrophic in isolation. But together, it erodes trust.

This is what the visual refers to as eroding structural advantage. The system still looks strong, but its realized advantage declines as flow maturity lags behind scale.

The decline is rarely attributed to flow. Instead, leaders respond with more structure, more tools, or more reporting.

Which accelerates the problem.

Flow Is Designed Systemically but Revealed Locally

A critical principle often overlooked in healthcare strategy is this:

Flow advantage is designed across the system, but it is revealed through everyday friction.

You do not observe flow directly. You observe its absence.

Every delay, unclear instruction, unnecessary handoff, or duplicated step is a signal that the system is not orchestrated end to end.

This is why last mile performance is such a powerful diagnostic. It exposes whether the organization is truly operating as a system or merely as a collection of optimized units.

The Nonlinear Nature of Flow Maturity

Improving flow is not a linear journey.

As systems transition from fragmented to orchestrated, realized advantage often dips before it rises. Legacy processes are dismantled before new ones stabilize. Accountability is clarified before it becomes effective.

This middle phase is uncomfortable.

Many organizations retreat here. They interpret temporary instability as failure and revert to familiar structural levers.

Those that persist redesign around flow rather than hierarchy.

They engineer transitions rather than departments. They assign ownership to journeys rather than functions. They remove ambiguity rather than add controls.

At this point, advantage begins to compound.

The Minimum Competitive Advantage Threshold

Every healthcare system operates relative to an implicit threshold.

Below it, patients tolerate the system. Above it, they trust it.

Trust changes behavior.

Patients return voluntarily. They comply more reliably. They consolidate their care. They refer others.

This threshold is not crossed through excellence in isolated moments. It is crossed through consistency across the last mile.

Consistency is the most underappreciated strategic asset in healthcare.

What Flow Advantage Actually Looks Like

The most advanced healthcare systems rarely advertise their sophistication.

They do not talk about flow. They do not celebrate orchestration. They do not brand their coordination.

They simply feel predictable.

Predictability is the outcome of hundreds of small design decisions made in favor of continuity over convenience and clarity over local optimization.

This is what the visual calls flow advantage. Not speed, but certainty. Not delight, but confidence.

The Strategic Implication

The last mile advantage is not a customer experience initiative. It is not a digital transformation project. It is not a service excellence program.

It is a system design problem.

Healthcare organizations that recognize this early build advantage that compounds quietly over time. Those that do not eventually find that their structural strength no longer protects them.

The next generation of healthcare systems will win not by building more, but by ensuring care moves cleanly, consistently, and without friction.

2.title: X-Shaped Strategists
2.page_title: (15) The Future of Healthcare Belongs to X-Shaped Strategists | LinkedIn
2.text:
The Healthcare Strategist
5,675 subscribers
Subscribed
The Healthcare Strategist Newsletter
The Future of Healthcare Belongs to X-Shaped Strategists
Mohanned Afeef
Group CSO, Saudi German Health | The Healthcare Strategist | Growth & Transformation in Health Systems
September 28, 2025
Why I Needed a New Lens

Every few weeks, I get the same question from younger colleagues and peers:

“How do I become a healthcare strategist?”

They’re often operators, clinicians, analysts, or product managers. Smart, ambitious, and deeply curious. But they feel stuck. Should they double down on operations? Should they get into digital health? Should they pursue finance, policy, or innovation?

The traditional career advice hasn’t changed in decades: pick a lane, go deep, and climb the ladder.

That worked when healthcare was simpler; when hospitals were smaller, systems were siloed, and strategy lived in corporate offices detached from care delivery. But that world is gone.

Today:

Care is converging across inpatient, outpatient, virtual, and home.
Technology blurs boundaries between physical and digital.
Operations are reinvented in real time to handle new challenges.
Healthcare economics determines what survives at scale.

In this world, staying in one lane makes you less relevant, not more. Strategy doesn’t emerge from silos. It emerges where silos collide.

That’s the premise behind what I call The X-Shaped Healthcare Strategist Framework.

It’s not a rigid model. It’s a compass. A way to think about your growth if you want to become a strategist who matters in tomorrow’s healthcare system.

Moving Beyond the T

For years, management thinkers used the metaphor of the T-shaped professional:

Depth in one discipline (the vertical stroke).
Breadth across others (the horizontal stroke).

It’s useful, but it doesn’t capture healthcare.

Why? Because healthcare doesn’t just need people who balance depth and breadth. It needs people who can stand at the intersections of disciplines; where different ways of thinking collide, overlap, and create something new.

That’s not a T. It’s an X.

Where a T suggests a balance of deep and broad, an X represents intersections. It’s in those intersections where strategists translate, integrate, and innovate.

The Four Domains of Healthcare Strategy

Every strategist begins in a home domain. These are the four pillars of the framework:

Care Design: Pathways, service design, patient journeys, experience.
Health Technology: Digital health, IT, AI, medical devices.
Healthcare Operations: Clinical delivery, safety, performance discipline.
Healthcare Economics: Financing, incentives, business and growth models.

None of these alone is “strategy.” But together, they form the raw material. And where they intersect, new strategist archetypes emerge.

Introducing the Framework

Each of these four domains; care design, health technology, healthcare operations, and healthcare economics, is vital. Many of us build our early careers by going deep into one of them. That’s how credibility is earned.

But here’s the problem: healthcare doesn’t move in straight lines.

A new care pathway fails without the right technology.
A promising technology fails without integration into workflows.
An efficient operation fails without financial sustainability.
And a clever economic model fails if it ignores patient experience.

No domain wins on its own. The real breakthroughs happen when they collide.

It’s a way to map how strategists evolve, from mastering a home domain, to bridging across two, to integrating three, and ultimately orchestrating all four. It’s more of a compass than a job description; a guide for how to grow into the kind of strategist healthcare actually needs.

The first step beyond specialization is becoming a Hybrid; a strategist forged at the intersection of two domains.

The Hybrids: Two-Domain Strategists

The first step beyond specialization is to become a hybrid; someone who works across two domains. Hybrids are translators. They bring disciplines together and unlock value that neither could deliver alone.

The Patient Access Strategist (Economics × Care Design)

They reimagine the “front door” of healthcare. Retail clinics, urgent care, pharmacy-led models, built to expand access while keeping economics sustainable.

The Digital Health Strategist (Care Design × Technology)

They make digital-first experiences seamless. For them, telehealth, virtual care, and engagement platforms are more than just add-ons, they’re integral parts of the patient journey.

The Care Delivery Strategist (Technology × Operations)

They re-engineer workflows. Think AI-enabled diagnostics, automated processes, and virtual command centers, all designed to make care more scalable and efficient.

The Care Efficiency Strategist (Operations × Economics)

They balance throughput, quality, and cost. They align incentives with operational discipline to ensure that systems remain sustainable without compromising care.

Hybrids are the bridge-builders. They’re often the first taste of strategy for operators, clinicians, or technologists moving beyond their home lane. But as challenges grow more complex, bridging two worlds isn’t enough. That’s where the Integrators emerge; strategists who operate across three domains at once.

The Integrators: Three-Domain Strategists

As complexity rises, healthcare needs more than bridges. It needs integrators; strategists who can synthesize across three domains at once.

The Care Model Strategist (Care Design × Technology × Operations)

They design entirely new models of care. From hospital-at-home to hybrid urgent care, they redefine delivery at scale.

The Health System Performance Strategist (Technology × Operations × Economics)

They maintain both clinical and financial sustainability. These strategists integrate digital tools with operational rigor and economic discipline.

The Health System Growth Strategist (Care Design × Operations × Economics)

They architect expansion. Whether adding sites, services, or networks, they align design and operations with growth economics.

The Population Health Strategist (Care Design × Technology × Economics)

They drive risk-aligned value. They harness tech and design to manage populations, align incentives with outcomes, and make risk-sharing contracts deliver healthier patients and healthier margins.

Integrators are the system-shapers. While they are able to connect domains, their role is to redesign how systems work. Yet even they stop short of the rarest archetype at the very center of the X: the Ecosystem Strategist, the orchestrator who aligns every domain into one coherent whole.

The Ecosystem Strategist: Orchestrating Across All Domains

At the very center of the X sits the rarest archetype: The Ecosystem Strategist.

Their role isn’t just to bridge or integrate; it’s to orchestrate.

Ecosystem strategists have lived across multiple intersections. They can speak the language of clinicians, technologists, operators, and financiers. And more importantly, they can align them around a single vision.

This is the strategist who redefine the game and in today’s healthcare landscape, that makes all the difference. 

Why This Matters Now

Three shifts make the X-Shaped Strategist urgent today:

Convergence of Care Models Boundaries between inpatient, outpatient, virtual, and home are dissolving. Strategists who can integrate across domains will define what hybrid care looks like.
Redefinition of Value Fee-for-service is giving way to outcomes and risk. The strategists who thrive will be those who can align design, economics, and operations.
Acceleration of Complexity Health systems are larger, more networked, and more data-driven than ever. Complexity isn’t the obstacle, it’s the playing field. The strategist’s job is to navigate it.

This is why healthcare strategists can no longer think in silos. The intersections are where impact is made.

A Compass for Your Career

Let me be clear: this framework isn’t about job titles. You won’t find a posting for “Ecosystem Strategist” on LinkedIn.

This is about capabilities.

Here’s how to use it as a compass:

Master your home domain. Depth first. Be credible.
Add one intersection. Pick an adjacent domain and build fluency.
Evolve into integration. Take on projects that force you to connect three domains.
Move toward orchestration. Learn to align all four.

Not everyone will want to move to the center. But those who do will future-proof their careers.

My Reflection

I didn’t design this framework in theory. It came from practice.

I’ve been lucky to move through multiple roles in healthcare: from frontline clinical care to operations, from technology to strategy and design. That journey taught me something simple:

The real impact happens in the spaces between disciplines. And between you and me, working at the intersections isn’t just the most meaningful work of your career, it’s also the most fun.

That’s why I built this framework. Not as a rigid model, but as a guide. To help those of you who ask me where to begin, how to grow, and how to future-proof your careers.

Because the future of healthcare doesn’t belong to those who stay in silos.

It belongs to those who learn to stand at the intersections.

3.title: Retention
3.page_title: (15) Retention: The Most Overlooked Growth Lever in Healthcare | LinkedIn
3.text:
The Healthcare Strategist
5,675 subscribers
Subscribed
Mohanned Afeef | The Healthcare Strategist
Retention: The Most Overlooked Growth Lever in Healthcare
Mohanned Afeef
Group CSO, Saudi German Health | The Healthcare Strategist | Growth & Transformation in Health Systems
August 24, 2025

In healthcare, most leaders talk about growth as if it were only about expansion. New hospitals. New specialties. New service lines. New partnerships.

But there is a quieter, more decisive battle being fought every day inside our health systems.

It isn’t about how many new patients walk in through the front door. It’s about how many never come back.

That battle is retention.

To understand why retention matters, we need to zoom out. Growth in healthcare does not begin with retention, it begins with the entire patient journey. Every health system moves through a cycle of attracting new patients, engaging them at the point of care, and then, if it is designed well, retaining them over time. I call this cycle the Care Loop.

The Care Loop: Attract → Engage → Retain

The Care Loop matters because it shows us the simple truth: retention is never an isolated problem. It is the outcome of everything that comes before it. Every patient moves through these steps:

Attract: The starting point. Patients enter the system through marketing, brand promise, or referrals. 
Engage: The first impression. Patients experience your clinical access, onboarding, and service delivery. 
Retain: The loyalty effect. The critical phase between engagement and retention is Trust Formation, where consistent care delivery translates into patient trust. 

Attracting patients or healthcare consumers sets expectations. Engagement delivers the first impression. Retention is earned when those two align and translate into trust. That is why we start here, because if the Care Loop breaks at the retention stage, the system stops compounding and starts leaking.

The Care Loop is not linear. It’s cyclical and compounding. Retention strengthens engagement. Engagement validates attracting new consumers. Attracting new consumers brings in new patients who feed back into the loop.

Without retention, the cycle collapses. With it, the Care Loop becomes a self-reinforcing engine of healthcare growth. But if retention is the most valuable stage of the loop, it’s also the one most health systems fail to master.

The Retention Problem

Here’s what actually happens in most systems.

Patient engagement decays. The curve bends downward as weeks and months pass. Some patients return for follow-up, but many do not. They drift. They get lost. They seek convenience elsewhere.

This is leakage. And leakage quietly erodes every effort you make to grow.

Think of the patient who came to your ER with a shoulder pain but never saw your orthopedic clinic again. Or the family that delivered their first child with you but went somewhere else for pediatric care.

Every one of those missed returns is not just lost revenue. It’s lost continuity, lost outcomes, lost trust.

In the graph below, both hospitals start at 100%. The difference is what happens next. Hospital A loses patients quickly, its curve is steep and fragile. Hospital B retains more patients, with a fatter tail, the foundation of sustainable growth.

Data is Illustrative

The consequences of leakage go far beyond revenue. Retention shapes outcomes, operations, and brand strength across the entire system.

Why Retention Matters

Retention is not a nice-to-have. It’s a multiplier.

Clinical: Patients who stay in your system experience continuity. That continuity reduces complications, improves adherence, and supports better long-term outcomes.
Operational: Stable, repeatable demand allows you to staff and resource more efficiently. Instead of spikes and troughs, you can plan.
Financial: Acquiring a new patient costs 5 – 7 times more than keeping one. The lifetime value of a returning patient is multiples higher than the value of a single visit.
Strategic: Retained patients become advocates. They recommend you, reinforce your brand, and attract others back into the loop.

Retention feeds every loop you are trying to build.

Retention isn’t luck. It signals trust, continuity, and engagement. Weak retention is silent leakage, patients don’t complain, they just disappear.

Data is Illustrative

And to understand the true impact, it helps to put retention into an equation.

The Growth Equation

Most health systems don’t fail at acquisition or attracting new consumers. They fail at retention.

The math is simple:

Growth = Acquisition + Retention – Leakage

Acquisition: new patients entering the system.
Retention: patients who return, increasing lifetime value.
Leakage: patients lost to competitors, lack of follow-up, or system gaps.

The problem? Almost every health system is obsessed with the first variable. Millions are spent on marketing campaigns, billboards, sponsorships. Far less is invested in the science of retention.

There is another way to write the equation. One that makes the leverage point even clearer:

Growth = (Attract + Engage) × Retain

Retention is not additive, it’s multiplicative. Without it, growth is linear and fragile. With it, growth compounds.

In the graph below, Hospital A looks like it’s winning, its volume curve is steeper. But when you track retention, the picture changes. Hospital B, with fewer new patients but stronger retention, is building durable growth. Thus, looking at growth only, can be a strategic blind spot.

Data is Illustrative

If retention is the multiplier of growth, then every friction point that disrupts it becomes a silent brake.

What Breaks Retention

Why do so many patients fail to return?

It’s rarely one dramatic failure. It’s friction. Small, compounding frictions.

No one followed up after discharge.
The next appointment was weeks away.
Insurance didn’t cover a key service.
The website was confusing.
A referral got lost.
The system never called back.

Individually, these are small. Together, they form an exit door.

The truth is, healthcare doesn’t need more front doors. It needs fewer exit doors. And the good news is retention can be designed, measured, and managed. That is why I built the RETAIN Framework for Health Systems.

The RETAIN Framework™ for Health Systems

Fixing retention isn’t about slogans or loyalty programs. It’s about designing the system to measure, manage, and continuously improve patient engagement and retention.

The RETAIN Framework™ for Health Systems; a six-step process to define, monitor, and embed retention across the enterprise.

Redefine: Clarify what “retention” means in each service line. Differentiate episodic vs. chronic pathways, and establish realistic expectations by specialty.
Establish: Build the baseline. Map patient lifetime value benchmarks, leakage points, and referral pathways. Without measurement, you cannot manage.
Target: Prioritize where improvement creates the most enterprise value. Align incentives and translate retention into financial and strategic goals.
Activate: Move from theory to patient-level action. Automate reminders, embed retention metrics into workflows, and intervene early when patients disengage.
Integrate: Make retention visible across the organization. Build dashboards, link KPIs to patient experience, and embed reporting into operational rhythms.
Nurture: Celebrate wins, refresh targets annually, and create continuous learning loops. Retention should become part of the culture DNA within the organization.

The RETAIN Framework™ for Health Systems turns retention into a discipline. It gives leaders an operating system to close leakage, grow loyalty, and transform retention from an invisible problem into a visible engine of growth.

Closing Thought

Healthcare doesn’t have a growth problem. It has a retention problem.

Retention is the quiet engine of healthcare growth. It compounds value, builds trust, and multiplies every other investment you make.

And in today’s market, retention strategies are not a luxury. They are a necessity.

Healthcare consumers have more options than ever before, digital entrants, retail clinics, specialized networks, even cross-border care. The real question is: why should they return to you?

The organizations that answer that question and design their systems around retention will never run in place again.

4.title: Loops vs Linear
4.page_title: (15) The Health System Strategy Framework: Why Loops Outperform Linear Plans | LinkedIn
4.text:
The Healthcare Strategist
5,675 subscribers
Subscribed
Mohanned Afeef | The Healthcare Strategist
The Health System Strategy Framework: Why Loops Outperform Linear Plans
Mohanned Afeef
Group CSO, Saudi German Health | The Healthcare Strategist | Growth & Transformation in Health Systems
August 10, 2025

In healthcare, most strategy still happens in a straight line. Leaders set annual targets, map a sequence of initiatives, and push them forward until the next planning cycle. But growth in complex systems rarely follows a neat sequence.

The most resilient health systems don’t just plan, they reinforce. Every decision, every relationship, every outcome strengthens the next. This is the logic behind the Health System Strategy Framework, a model built to keep strategy in motion, not on a slide.

It draws its inspiration from the Health System Flywheel, my earlier model showing how consumers, providers, and payers can reinforce each other in a self-sustaining growth engine. If you haven’t seen that breakdown, I recommend reading it first, then returning here to see how the Strategy Framework takes those dynamics and turns them into a practical blueprint.

At its core, the framework recognizes that all sustainable growth follows the same flow:

Create Value by building trust and preference with consumers.
Deliver Value through measurable, high-quality outcomes.
Capture Value by aligning with payers to expand access and ensure returns.

These principles are embedded into three reinforcing loops; the Care Loop, the Outcome Loop, and the Value Loop, that together replace the fragility of one-way plans with the compounding momentum of a living system.

Why Loops Work: The Flywheel Concept

The idea of self-reinforcing loops isn’t new. Business strategist Jim Collins popularized the flywheel concept in his book Good to Great. His research showed that the most enduringly successful companies didn’t rely on a single significant breakthrough. Instead, they built systems where small, consistent wins reinforced each other, creating unstoppable momentum.

Think of a heavy flywheel in a factory. It’s hard to push at first. But with each turn, it stores more energy. Eventually, the momentum carries it forward with less effort, and that’s when real acceleration happens.

Amazon famously used a flywheel to become the world’s largest online retailer. The logic was simple:

Lower prices attracted more customers.
More customers attracted more third-party sellers.
More sellers expanded product selection.
Better selection improved customer experience.
Which led back to more customers, enabling economies of scale and lower prices again.

That’s a loop. And it’s why Amazon’s growth wasn’t just fast, it was compounding. Every element fed the others. The genius of Amazon’s model wasn’t just in the individual steps. The genius was in how each step strengthened the next, and that’s precisely the kind of compounding effect healthcare needs.

The Macro Loop: A Flywheel for Health Systems

At the center of the Health System Strategy Framework is the Macro Loop, connecting three core stakeholders: 

 Healthcare Consumers → Talented Providers → Diverse Payers → back to Consumers.

The blueprint for creating, delivering, and capturing value in healthcare

Here’s how it works:

More consumers choosing your system attract and retain more talented providers (supply follows demand).
More talented providers improve clinical quality, reputation, and service differentiation, making the system more attractive to payers.
More diverse payers expand coverage options and reduce financial risk, making the system accessible to more consumers.
Which brings in more consumers, and the loop spins faster.

Each of the three micro loops plays a distinct role in this value cycle:

The Care Loop creates value.
The Outcome Loop delivers value.
The Value Loop captures value.

When designed well, each loop strengthens the others. When designed poorly, each weak point drags the others down. The Macro Loop is the structural foundation for sustainable growth, the equivalent of Amazon’s flywheel, but for a health system. While the Macro Loop shows the big picture, real momentum is powered by these three smaller engines; the micro loops.

The Care Loop: Building Trust That Compounds

The Care Loop is the consumer-facing growth engine. It moves through Attract → Engage → Retain, creating a flywheel where every positive care experience fuels the next patient decision and interaction.

Creating Value: building trust and shaping preference through exceptional care experiences

The real power of the Care loop lies in how it shapes preference. In healthcare, that preference is built through trust, and trust is most visible when patients recommend you to others. A strong Care Loop turns exceptional care into word of mouth, and word of mouth into the most credible form of marketing there is.

Creating value here means making care more accessible, personal, and responsive so that each interaction deepens trust and strengthens the relationship. The strategic questions to ask here are about whether you're positioned for access, convenience, and trust? Whether your care experiences stand out in a way that makes people choose you and keep choosing you?

When the Care Loop is strong, marketing spend becomes more efficient, acquisition costs drop, and growth is fueled by reputation, not just promotion.

However, winning patients is one thing. Delivering consistent, exceptional results is another, and that’s where the Outcome Loop comes in.

The Outcome Loop: Differentiating Through Results

The Outcome Loop powers the provider side of the equation. It’s where Anticipate → Coordinate → Optimize comes together to deliver clinical excellence at scale.

Delivering Value: achieving measurable, defensible, and scalable clinical outcomes

Delivering value means consistently achieving outcomes that matter, outcomes that are measurable, defensible, and scalable. The strategic questions are clear: Do you have the operational discipline to deliver exceptional results every time? Are those results strong enough to set you apart from competitors? And are you turning outcome feedback into foresight, improving before problems surface?

A well-run Outcome Loop earns trust not just from patients, but from payers and partners who see your results as reliable and repeatable.

Great outcomes are powerful, but they can’t sustain a system on their own; they need to be converted into leverage with payers, which is the focus of the Value Loop.

The Value Loop: Turning Performance Into Leverage

The Value Loop connects providers and payers in a self-reinforcing cycle of Align → Secure → Leverage.

Capturing Value: aligning with payers to secure access, scale, and resilience

Capturing value here means turning performance into scale, financial resilience, and access for more patients. The strategic questions are evident: Are you aligned with the right payer mix; commercial, public, employer-based, or government-funded? Do your contracts reflect the value you deliver? Are you using your performance and scale to negotiate better terms, expand coverage, and attract the right populations?

When the Value Loop spins, financial resilience improves, growth becomes sustainable, and innovation has the funding to scale.

But these loops, Care, Outcome, and Value loops, don’t operate in a vacuum. These loops rely on a set of enablers that keep them spinning, and spinning faster.

The Enablers: The Framework’s Hidden Power

The loops accelerate when three enablers work together and are measured as rigorously as any clinical or financial outcome.

Data and Intelligence: Turn signals into foresight so decisions happen early and close to the action. Unify clinical, operational, and financial data into a single source of truth, focus on leading indicators, and build feedback loops for every initiative.

Done well, this improves patient targeting in care, quality tracking in Outcome, and performance reporting in value. Weak data leads to delays, debates over numbers, and missed trends.

Leadership and Culture: Align people on a few critical priorities and hold to a clear operating rhythm. This means governance that empowers, talent systems that reward loop performance, and a culture where teams escalate issues quickly and leaders coach.

Strong culture creates consistent Care experiences, disciplined coordination in Outcome, and reliability that payers trust in value.

Capital and Technology: Direct investment toward capacities that most increase loop velocity, supported by technology that is interoperable, reliable, and easy to use. This reduces friction in care, raises reliability in Outcome, and strengthens financial performance in value. Misaligned investment slows momentum and scatters focus.

When these enablers are coherent, the loops compound rather than compete, and the system begins to move with less push and more pull.

Why Loops Beat Lists

Linear strategies push effort in a straight line until the project ends. Loops recycle that effort into the next success, creating momentum that’s hard to stop.

In healthcare, where complexity, fragmentation, and high stakes can stall progress, loops are the difference between incremental improvement and exponential impact. Big tech learned this decades ago. Healthcare is just catching up.

Looking Ahead

This framework is still evolving, and I’m building it in public.

The fundamentals are strong, but the applications will grow as more leaders test and adapt them.

In my upcoming work, I’ll unpack each loop and enabler in detail, including the mechanics, metrics, and case examples that turn this from a visual into a working strategy.

For now, I’ll leave you with this:

A fixed, once-a-year strategy with a clear start and end belongs to another era.

Today’s healthcare systems need strategies that are dynamic, data-informed, and continuously aligned; strategies that learn and adapt as fast as the world around them.

That’s what loops do. They turn strategy from an annual exercise into a living system that creates, delivers, and captures value every single day.

5.title: Health System Flywheel
5.page_title: (15) The Hidden Engine Behind Healthcare Systems' Growth: Inside the Health System Flywheel | LinkedIn
5.text:
The Healthcare Strategist
5,675 subscribers
Subscribed
The Hidden Engine Behind Healthcare Systems' Growth: Inside the Health System Flywheel
Mohanned Afeef
Group CSO, Saudi German Health | The Healthcare Strategist | Growth & Transformation in Health Systems
June 18, 2025

Over the past three editions of The Healthcare Strategist, we’ve used the Health System Flywheel to decode how CVS Health, Al Habib Medical Group, and Oak Street Health each achieved sustainable growth. 

While each organization followed its own path, their momentum was powered by the same underlying structure, a system where consumers, providers, and payers reinforce each other.

In this edition, we shift focus.

Instead of mapping how the flywheel shows up in others, we’re breaking it down from the inside out, loop by loop. We’ll explore how the Care, Outcome, and Value Loops form the foundation of the flywheel, what makes each loop move faster (or stall), and why the Macro Loop is not the starting point of growth but its natural result.

Growth in healthcare isn't a straight line. It's not guaranteed by expansion, capital, or size. And it doesn't come from reacting faster than your competitors. Sustainable growth happens when every part of the system reinforces the others; consumers, providers, and payers working in sync.

This is the idea behind the Health System Growth Flywheel.

Let's break it down.

The Macro Loop: When Growth Becomes Self-Sustaining

At the highest level, the flywheel operates as a reinforcing cycle:

More Healthcare Consumers

→ generate more demand and revenue, which

→ enables investment in more talented providers, who

→ deliver better clinical outcomes, which

→ build payer trust, leading to

→ more diverse payers, who

→ expand access, bringing in

→ even more consumers.

Each step reinforces the next. However, this loop doesn't move unless the underlying mechanisms are functioning correctly. The Macro Loop is a reflection of system momentum when growing at scale.

The Macro Loop requires activation and acceleration by three different micro loops that align consumers, providers, and payers within this self-sustaining system.

The Real Engine: Three Micro Loops

Behind every successful system lies a foundation of aligned micro loops. Each one focuses on a core relationship within the ecosystem:

Consumers and Providers.
Providers and Payers.
Payers and Consumers.

When these loops spin independently, a system grows.

But when they spin together, a system compounds.

1. The Care Loop

This loop is driven by trust and experience.

It centers on how well a system builds relationships with its consumers through care that feels personal, responsive, and accessible.

The priority here is to build a stronger patient-provider relationship.

When patients feel they are seen, heard, and valued, they return, and not only that, but they also bring others with them.

The Care Loop is where preference is shaped. It's also where loyalty begins.

Patients don't remember your billing system. They remember whether the doctor looked them in the eye, whether the nurse followed up, and whether they felt like a person rather than a case.

Every touchpoint, from scheduling and signage to bedside manner, will either accelerate or decelerate the loop.

A system with a strong Care Loop will not only grow in visits; it will also grow in relationships.

2. The Outcome Loop

This loop is driven by performance and credibility. It reflects the system's ability to deliver measurable results, not just activity, but outcomes that matter. At its core, this loop answers a payer's unspoken question: Can we trust this provider to deliver?

Systems that understand this loop will not only measure quality; they will operationalize it. They make outcomes a language that payers speak fluently.

The Outcome Loop is what turns clinical excellence into strategic leverage. And it's where the bridge between care delivery and financial sustainability is built.

3. The Value Loop

This loop is driven by access and reach. Even the best care is irrelevant if it's out of reach.

The Value Loop determines how many people your system can realistically serve based on coverage, convenience, and cost.

A system with a strong Value Loop expands its footprint without building new hospitals. 

It does so by aligning with more payers, meeting more consumers where they are, and designing coverage models that make sense for today's workforce and population.

This loop connects payers' diversity with accessibility, serving as the mechanism for the flywheel's expansion.

If the Care Loop is how you earn patients' trust, and the Outcome Loop is how you earn payers' trust, the Value Loop is how you scale both.

Loop Accelerators and Decelerators

Every loop spins faster or slower depending on the forces acting upon it. These forces either accelerate momentum or introduce friction.

Leaders who recognize these dynamics early can avoid costly missteps and unlock compounding growth sooner.

Care Loop Accelerators and Decelerators

Care Loop accelerators are factors that enhance the patient's emotional, relational, and practical experience, making them more likely to return, refer, and trust the system. 

Decelerators, on the other hand, are forces that create friction, confusion, or disconnection.

Accelerators:

Seamless access to care (booking, check-in, navigation).
Empathetic communication at every touchpoint.
Clear post-visit follow-up.
Culturally relevant and personalized interactions.
Continuity with care teams over time.

Decelerators:

Long wait times, difficult scheduling.
Cold, transactional encounters.
Lack of follow-through or confusion after discharge.
Inconsistent provider relationships.
Administrative complexity (billing, records, authorizations).

Outcome Loop Accelerators and Decelerators

Outcome Loop accelerators are system capabilities that enhance a health system's ability to consistently deliver, measure, and communicate high-value clinical results. Decelerators are structural or process-related barriers that weaken the translation of care delivery into measurable performance.

Accelerators:

Transparent, benchmarked outcomes reporting.
Real-time data collection and feedback.
Clinical and administrative alignment on quality goals.
Strong referral coordination across specialties.

Decelerators:

Data or measurement gaps.
Misaligned incentives between departments.
Quality programs that exist only on paper.
Fragmented patient journeys that erode accountability.

Value Loop Accelerators and Decelerators

Value Loop accelerators are design features that increase the breadth and depth of access across payer types, geographies, and care models. Decelerators are limitations in access, affordability, or reach that prevent potential consumers from entering or staying in the system.

Accelerators:

Broad payer mix and inclusive network design.
Revenue cycle operations that reduce friction and accelerate reimbursements.
Multichannel service delivery (in-person, virtual, home).
Accessible benefit structures.
Public-private partnerships or employer-sponsored access.

Decelerators:

Network restrictions or payer concentration risk.
High claim denial rates or delayed reimbursements.
Uniform pricing models that don't fit all populations.
Physical and geographic barriers to care.
Lack of financial transparency or digital tools.

Understanding these levers helps systems tune each loop for maximum momentum. It's not always about doing more. Often, it's about removing the friction that's already there.

Alignment Creates Acceleration

Each loop matters on its own. But the transformation happens when they work together.

Care drives demand.

Outcomes build credibility.

Value expands access.

When all three are aligned, the Macro Loop stops being a strategy slide and starts being the way your system grows.

The Macro Loop Is a Reflection of System Integrity

Health systems that chase scale without designing the loops underneath are building on a fragile foundation.

Growth doesn't fail because of market conditions. It fails when the system lacks reinforcement.

The Care Loop reflects how well the system connects with consumers.

The Outcome Loop reflects how well it performs.

The Value Loop reflects how well it expands reach.

If those three loops aren't active and aligned, no amount of investment or expansion will create real momentum.

Final Thought

Growth is not the starting point. It's the signal that the system is working.

What we call growth is just the external expression of internal alignment.

Trust. Performance. Access.

That's the system. That's the architecture. That's the flywheel.

You don't grow a health system by scaling what's broken.

You grow it by designing what works and letting the system do the rest.

6.title: Oak Street Health
6.page_title: (15) The Quiet Reinvention of Primary Care: Inside Oak Street Health's Growth Model | LinkedIn
6.text:
The Healthcare Strategist
5,675 subscribers
Subscribed
The Healthcare Strategist Newsletter
The Quiet Reinvention of Primary Care: Inside Oak Street Health's Growth Model
Mohanned Afeef
Group CSO, Saudi German Health | The Healthcare Strategist | Growth & Transformation in Health Systems
June 3, 2025

Welcome to the Third Edition of the Healthcare Strategist. 

In the first two editions, we explored two very different engines of healthcare growth, the retail-powered flywheel of CVS Health, and the fully integrated delivery system of Al Habib Medical Group in Saudi Arabia.

This time, we shift geographies again. But remain focused on a new growth story.

Oak Street Health offers something rare: a system designed entirely around outcomes, not activity and a model that may hold quiet lessons for anyone trying to make value-based care work, anywhere in the world.

It began with a simple premise:

If you had to take full financial responsibility for a patient's health, how would you redesign care?

Not just tweak the workflow. Not just digitize the clipboard. But rebuild the entire operating system of primary care, from who you hire to how often patients visit to what happens when they leave.

In 2012, Oak Street Health opened its first clinic on the South Side of Chicago. A decade later, it was operating in over 25 states, serving hundreds of thousands of patients, mostly lower-income, elderly individuals who were often overlooked by the traditional healthcare system. And then, in 2023, CVS Health acquired Oak Street for $10.6 billion.

It was the clearest signal yet that value-based care had crossed a threshold: value-based care evolved from a model that could work to one that could scale. Oak Street's real achievement isn't the acquisition; rather, it is the design of its model.

A Model Designed for Risk

Most healthcare systems are shifting toward a value-based approach. But Oak Street Health designed it.

Every element, from clinic size to staffing ratios to visit frequency, reflects one central truth: when you're financially accountable for outcomes, your margin depends on foresight, not activity.

In short, Oak Street is not built for throughput. Oak Street has been designed for a higher level of care coordination, care continuity, and, most importantly, clinical accountability, where every encounter is measured not by volume but by its impact on long-term health outcomes.

A System That Compounds

At the core of Oak Street's performance is a system design that reinforces itself over time. It scales by getting tighter, more efficient, more coordinated, and more trusted. This kind of growth doesn't happen linearly. It happens in loops.

This Oak Street growth flywheel framework identifies the interconnected loops between consumers, providers, and payers in truly integrated systems, revealing a four-part flywheel.

The Growth Engine: Oak Street Macro Loop

Patients → Clinical Teams → Payers → Patients

The more patients Oak Street attracts, the more data it collects, the more tightly it can manage risk. That success attracts clinicians who want to practice upstream care. Stronger teams deliver stronger outcomes, which attract and retain value-based payer contracts, bringing in more patients and allowing Oak Street to expand into new ZIP codes.

The macro loop is powered by three directional micro loops: the care loop, the outcomes loop, and the value loop, each reinforcing a different part of the system.

1. The Care Loop: Driven by Consumer Preference

When a Medicare Advantage patient walks into an Oak Street clinic, they are met not with transactional care but a social ecosystem:

Free rides to the clinics.
Walkable access.
Consistent care teams.
Rooms are designed for community, not volume.
Follow-up support is available after hours by phone.

This design has gone beyond delivering an excellent patient experience. It's a strategic investment in stickiness.

The patients Oak Street serves often carry a legacy of fragmented care. Rebuilding trust, and most importantly, habit, is crucial for achieving positive outcomes. 

This is precisely the reason why the average patient is seen eight times a year, with each visit lasting at least 30 minutes. Over time, that engagement becomes data, relationships, and early intervention.

Trust is not a soft metric. At Oak Street, it's a risk-management asset.

As the care loop spins, it allows Oak Street to invest in hiring talented professionals. From physicians to advanced practice nurse practitioners, behavioral health professionals, community health workers, scribes, and care coordinators, all work in sync to ensure the achievement of positive health outcomes. This team kicks in the second loop, the outcomes loop.

2. The Outcomes Loop: Driven by Exceptional Care

In most systems, better outcomes require constant intervention from overextended providers. 

At Oak Street, outcomes are the product of system design.

Each team is supported by:

Real-time risk alerts through the Canopy platform.
Daily clinical huddles for coordination.
Embedded behavioral health and pharmacy support.
Community Health Workers who close social gaps before they become medical crises.

The result is beyond mere statistics. It's actuarial:

51% fewer ER visits.
44% fewer hospital admissions.
Higher control rates in diabetes, hypertension, and depression.

Any value-based program that can achieve such excellent outcomes will not struggle to find a diverse set of payers ready to enroll their members. For Oak Street Health, these results spun their Value Loop.

3. The Value Loop: Driven by Payer Confidence

Oak Street documents performance at scale and payers notice.

By capturing risk accurately (thanks to high visit frequency), and avoiding avoidable costs (thanks to care coordination and continuity), Oak Street becomes a low-volatility partner in high-risk markets. That builds trust and enables deeper payer relationships.

Today, Oak Street contracts across:

Medicare Advantage plans (Humana, Aetna, WellCare, etc).
ACO REACH (CMS' redesigned direct contracting program).
And retail partners through CVS' ecosystem.

With each payer contract comes a new market, a new set of patients, and a new opportunity to loop the flywheel again.

The Strategic Payoff: CVS + Oak Street & the 10.6 $ Billion Acquisition

CVS didn't buy clinics. It bought a carefully designed and brilliantly executed model of care targeting high-risk population in underserved areas of the U.S healthcare ecosystem.

By reinventing primary and preventative health, Oak Street became the physical delivery layer in a vertically integrated system that now includes:

CVS retail stores.
Aetna health plans.
Signify Health's at-home services.

With co-branded clinics opening inside CVS stores, Oak Street has evolved from a care model into a national platform for risk-based care delivery, combining retail access, primary care continuity, and payer control.

This is what it looks like when value-based care goes from pilot project to platform.

The Takeaway

We should not let the future of healthcare happen to us. Oak Street Health just proved that we can design it. We can mold it. We can make it work for patients, providers, and payers. 

The Oak Street Health story has evolved from delivering better visits and improving outcomes to becoming about system design, scale, and what becomes possible when we think in first principles, focusing on removing complexity, simplifying processes, and carving out a space in a crowded healthcare market.

Most health systems are still trying to make value-based care coexist with fee-for-service operations. 

Oak Street proved you can build a model where everything points in the same direction.

That's a reinvention.

And it may be the most important operating model in American primary care today.

7.title: Al Habib
7.page_title: (15) Al Habib Didn't Just Build Hospitals. It Built a System that Scales. | LinkedIn
7.text:
The Healthcare Strategist
5,675 subscribers
Subscribed
Al Habib Didn't Just Build Hospitals. It Built a System that Scales.
Mohanned Afeef
Group CSO, Saudi German Health | The Healthcare Strategist | Growth & Transformation in Health Systems
May 27, 2025

In our first edition, we explored how CVS Health, a neighborhood pharmacy chain, evolved into one of the largest healthcare organizations in the world.

CVS Health transformation didn't begin with infrastructure or facilities. It began with a deeper understanding of the individual; the patient. With that understanding came alignment between the core actors of healthcare: consumers, providers, and payers.

CVS built a flywheel that fed itself. And as that system turned, it created momentum.

This week, we turn our attention to a very different geography and a seemingly opposite story: one that turned infrastructure into a platform.

Few healthcare systems in the Middle East have redefined scale and integration like Al Habib Medical Group (HMG). What began in Riyadh in the 1990s as a single outpatient complex has become a regional platform spanning 25+ medical facilities, 35 pharmacies, 4,500+ physicians, and 60+ sub-specialties.

Today, HMG touches the lives of over 7.4 million patients annually, supported by a workforce of 24,000+ staff and a footprint that stretches across Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Bahrain.

In addition to being the first private healthcare provider to list on the Saudi stock exchange, HMG also is one of the most operationally and financially efficient. With 3,300+ hospital beds, a dividend payout of 72.1%, and 1.67 billion SAR in declared dividends, it delivers top-tier returns while advancing clinical capacity and digital health infrastructure.

From virtual academies issuing over 17,800 certificates, to LEED-certified hospitals, to a growing connected care ecosystem, HMG has built more than facilities; HMG has engineered a system that scales.

Yet, despite its massive scale, what makes Al Habib compelling isn't this infrastructure but the way its system moves.

Underneath the hospital wings and command centers lies something more dynamic: a set of reinforcing loops that, much like CVS, link the patient to the provider and the payer in a closed and continuous system of growth, a flywheel designed with one goal in mind, that it keeps on spinning.

To understand how Al Habib has engineered its growth, we start where every system must: with patients' choice.

HMG Macro Loop: Where Patient Choice Becomes the System's First Mover

Like most great systems, Al Habib's growth begins with the choice of the patient.

Patients don't walk into Al Habib hospitals because they are the newest or the most visible. They walk in because they've heard stories of outcomes, attentiveness, technology that works, and teams that respond.

This reputation, hard-earned over decades of consistent delivery, has created a pull effect drawing in more patients, more families, more referrals, and more trust.

That trust becomes volume. And volume, in a system designed to reinvest, becomes something far more powerful: capability.

Revenue is not stockpiled. It is routed back into the system, where it is used to recruit high-caliber physicians and nurses, expand specialty services, and modernize digital platforms.

The system, in turn, attracts a more talented clinical workforce, professionals who see Al Habib as a platform to practice medicine at the highest level.

With better teams come better outcomes. Those outcomes become signals to patients, physicians, and, most crucially, payers.

Insurers, whether commercial giants like Tawuniya and Bupa or regional funds backed by government, respond to performance.

When outcomes are consistent, and care is coordinated, payers take notice. They begin to direct patients, expand coverage, and enter partnerships that are not just transactional but strategic.

And with broader coverage comes broader access and more patients can choose Al Habib. The loop closes, only to spin faster with each cycle.

More Consumers → More Demand → More Revenue → More High-Caliber Medical Workforce → Better Outcomes → More Payers → Expanded Access → More Consumers.

This is not a linear chain. It is a regenerative loop.

This is Al Habib's Growth Flywheel, whose center is not infrastructure but a carefully designed platform.

As HMG's macro loop spins, drawing in more patients and reinforcing system-wide momentum, something else starts to happen. The patients begin to prefer HMG over others, and a Micro Loop, the Care Loop, is born.

The Care Loop: Where Familiarity Transforms into Loyalty

As the macro loop gains speed, a micro loop begins to operate inside it, quieter but no less important. It is here, in the day-to-day moments of care, that Al Habib earns something deeper than volume: preference.

The Care Loop is not about advertising or branding. It is about consistency. A mother who finds that appointments for her child can be scheduled in seconds. A diabetic patient whose lab results appear automatically on the app; no follow-up is required. A family that returns, again and again, not because they have to, but because they want to.

Al Habib has not simply digitized the care experience. It has choreographed it.

From AI-enabled triage systems to centralized scheduling, from multi-specialty integration to real-time clinical decision support, every layer of the patient journey has been engineered to remove friction.

The system is not designed around services but around people, their needs, time, and trust.

As more patients experience this seamlessness, word spreads. The brand becomes a story, and the story becomes the promise. That reputation drives demand, and demand drives consistency.

This is how the Care Loop spins. Consistency builds loyalty. 

However, loyalty alone isn't enough to sustain a healthcare system. In a sector built on evidence, only one thing earns long-term credibility: outcomes.

The Outcomes Loop: Where Systems Make Excellence Repeatable

In healthcare, trust can only go so far. Systems are ultimately judged by what they deliver.

The Outcomes Loop begins not in marketing reports but in ICUs, operating rooms, and clinical quality measures. Here, Al Habib distinguishes itself through clinical excellence.

Consider the Tele-ICU Command Center, the largest of its kind globally, which monitors and supports nearly 800 critical care beds across the Gulf. Or the HMG Medical Academy, where continuous professional development is not optional but embedded, ensuring a pipeline of high-caliber physicians, surgeons, nurses, and allied health professionals. Layer on top of that a digitally mature ecosystem: interconnected lab networks, AI-enhanced radiology suites, and EMR systems that reduce clinical variability and sharpen diagnostic precision. Surround it all with Centers of Excellence spanning key specialties, and what emerges is a learning, self-improving system built for scale and outcomes.

These are not isolated initiatives. They are interdependent parts of a system built to produce one thing: consistent outcomes.

It's this consistency that unlocks the next layer of growth. Once a system can prove performance, it begins to speak the language of payers.

The Value Loop: Where Alignment Creates Scale

Payers are not moved by anecdotes. They are moved by patterns and the ability to predict and control cost, risk, and recovery.

The Value Loop is where Al Habib's clinical credibility meets commercial alignment. Payers gain confidence when outcomes are predictable, and patient journeys are coordinated and continuous.

The result?

Insurers expand their relationship with the system, coverage is extended, and consumers gain access to a care network that feels more like a pathway. 

As more payers participate, the system grows in coherence. But with each new payer, the flywheel tightens, pulling in more patients, reinforcing the care loop, strengthening the outcome loop, and amplifying the value loop once more.

As these loops reinforce each other, the outcomes, and value loops, it becomes tempting to point to HMG's massive infrastructures as the reason for its success. But HMG's infrastructure is not the cause; it is the consequence.

Infrastructure as Platform

It would be easy to mistake Al Habib's growth for an infrastructure story.

After all, few health systems have built so much, so quickly, and so effectively.

But infrastructure, in this model, is not the cause of growth. It is the consequence of it. Or, more precisely, it's the accelerant.

Buildings don't move flywheels.

People do. Systems do. Outcomes do.

Without a high-functioning care system, a 500-bed hospital is an expense. But within HMG's ecosystem, it's a growth catalyst.

Al Habib has built a platform upon which its flywheel spins faster, its loops operate at scale, and its core logic is applied across new geographies and service lines with consistency and excellence.

Closing Thought

CVS Health and Al Habib Medical Group could not be more different in their origins. One began with convenience retail. The other with clinical ambition. One minimized infrastructure. The other built it at scale. But both converged on the same insight:

Growth in healthcare doesn't start with infrastructure. It starts with patients' trust.

That trust is earned through care, reinforced through outcomes, and expanded through payers' alignment.

And when those elements come together in a system designed to move, a flywheel begins to spin.

In the case of Al Habib, that flywheel is no longer theoretical.

It's national, regional, and, perhaps soon global.

8.title: CVS Health
8.page_title: (15) How CVS Health Became a Healthcare Giant without Building Hospitals | LinkedIn
8.text:
The Healthcare Strategist
5,675 subscribers
Subscribed
How CVS Health Became a Healthcare Giant without Building Hospitals
Mohanned Afeef
Group CSO, Saudi German Health | The Healthcare Strategist | Growth & Transformation in Health Systems
May 20, 2025

Welcome to the first edition of The Healthcare Strategist. This newsletter explores bold ideas for those building the future of healthcare; grounded in strategy, shaped by real-world transformation, and guided by a simple belief: that sustainable growth starts with trust, not infrastructure.

Let’s begin.

Few people would have predicted in the early 2000s that CVS, the neighborhood pharmacy known for 30-foot-long receipts, would become the second-largest healthcare company in the world.

CVS was a chain of convenience stores with a side hustle in prescriptions. Yet, by 2024, CVS Health stood just behind UnitedHealth Group in scale, influence, and revenue, which exceeded $357 billion.

CVS operated one of the largest insurance portfolios in the U.S., employed tens of thousands of healthcare providers, and delivered care to over 100 million Americans through its clinics, telehealth services, and newly acquired primary care networks.

This transformation didn't happen by accident. It happened because CVS did something that most traditional health systems still resist. It paid attention to healthcare consumers.

CVS recognized that people weren't just patients; they were shoppers, workers, parents, and caregivers. They had preferences. They valued convenience. They wanted clarity. And they were tired of a system that felt cold and indifferent.

Instead of competing with hospitals, CVS sidestepped them. It began offering basic primary care services through walk-in clinics inside its stores. It added pharmacists trained in counseling and chronic disease support. It built digital platforms that made refilling prescriptions, checking coverage, and scheduling visits easier.

Today, it is one of the largest healthcare companies in the world.

And it achieved all of this without building a single hospital.

This transformation wasn't luck. It resulted from a strategy, but not the kind we usually discuss in healthcare. It wasn't a five-year master plan or a series of pilot projects. 

It was more organic; a rhythm, a pattern, a flywheel.

As I studied how CVS grew, I began noticing something subtle but powerful: a self-reinforcing cycle of momentum, a loop that connected the three major arteries of healthcare: the consumer, the provider, and the payer.

Each turn nourished the next, like a system circulating energy and strength through its design.

And once I saw it, I started seeing it everywhere.

The Core Growth Loop: A System That Feeds Itself

At the heart of CVS's evolution is a deceptively simple loop:

More Consumers → More Talented Providers → More Diverse Payers → More Consumers.

This wasn't a linear path. It was connective tissue that kept regenerating.

Each link strengthened the next. When more consumers chose your system, it signaled demand, and as any economist will tell you, supply follows demand. The result? A magnet for top-tier providers who wanted to practice where patients were already choosing to go. 

Better providers led to better outcomes. Those outcomes built payer confidence. Payers expanded access and coverage, and that access drew in more consumers.

CVS didn't just stumble into this loop. It engineered it.

But this core loop didn't spin on its own. It needed smaller, faster cycles within it, loops that would generate the momentum to keep the entire system in motion. The first of those loops formed at the point of care; the Care Loop.

The Care Loop: Where Trust Becomes Demand

CVS started earning consumer trust by embedding care into the fabric of daily life.

Pharmacists were trained to support chronic conditions. MinuteClinics made walk-in care part of the errand run. Digital platforms offered low-friction scheduling, refills, and navigation.

It didn't try to outdo the hospitals. Instead, it built something more intimate and immediate, closing the gap between health needs and real life.

That presence became a preference. Preference became loyalty. Loyalty drove demand. And demand pulled in talent who wanted to work in systems that patients trusted.

This is where the first internal loop took shape: the Care Loop (Consumer Loop).

But trust alone isn't enough. To truly transform the system, CVS needed to prove that its model could deliver results, not just convenience. That's where the next loop emerged.

The Outcome Loop: Where Performance Becomes Leverage

CVS didn't stop at convenience. It doubled down on performance.

Its decentralized care model wasn't just more accessible; it proved to be more effective. Patients got in sooner. They stayed on therapy longer. And they avoided complications that would have sent them to more acute settings.

These outcomes weren't a side effect. They were the product. And they gave CVS leverage. Not in the boardroom, but at the point of partnership. 

With results in hand, CVS earned the right to co-create benefit structures and to speak the language of payers: risk, results, and return.

This is where the second internal loop clicked into place: the Outcome Loop.

However, performance wasn't enough. Scale required alignment, and alignment needed deeper control of care's financial and structural foundations. That moment came with a single, defining move.

The Value Loop: Where Alignment Drives Scale

The 2018 acquisition of Aetna marked a pivotal moment, not just because of its scale but also because of its synchrony.

With Aetna, CVS became a payer, a provider, and a platform.

Insurance products could now be designed based on real-world care insights.

Care teams operated with less friction. Consumers experienced a smoother journey, from coverage to care to recovery.

It was the loop between data, dollars, and delivery.

This was the Value Loop, a tight circuit that brought clarity to a system often blurred by complexity.

With all three loops spinning, care, outcomes, and value, the whole system came into view. What CVS had built wasn't just a service or a network; it was a growth engine!

The Full Picture: A Growth Engine in Motion

Once all three loops were in motion, the system began to accelerate.

Each loop didn't just feed the next but amplified each other. 

Consumers drove volume, providers delivered outcomes, and payers unlocked scale. 

CVS didn't grow by chance; it grew by closing the loop. CVS grew by design!




CVS built alignment where others built departments. CVS created continuity where others created silos. In a fragmented healthcare landscape, that alignment became a rare and formidable advantage.

The Future of Growth is Consumer-Driven

CVS did not grow by building more infrastructure. It grew by creating connective tissue between the three major actors in healthcare: the consumer, the provider, and the payer. It did what the best systems do intuitively; it closed the loop.

For consumers, it simplified access. It made healthcare feel less like an institution and more like an everyday service.
For providers, it created new pathways to reach patients, supported by data and technology.
For payers, it offered visibility, outcomes, and cost control; all in one network.

For decades, hospitals have grown by adding beds, expanding service lines, and lobbying for contracts. Growth was an internal pursuit: build more, hire more, and bill more. 

But what if the most resilient growth doesn't come from what you build, but from who chooses you?

That's the quiet insight behind CVS's rise. 

Growth didn't start with infrastructure. 

It started with the individual.

The shopper. The parent. The patient.

And once that flywheel began to spin, everything else followed.

In future editions of The Healthcare Strategist, I'll explore how other systems are finding their flywheel and how leaders can build not just programs, but momentum.

The systems that will lead tomorrow won't be defined by the size of their infrastructure, but by the strength of their consumers' affinity.

They will be the ones that patients return to, not out of necessity, but by choice.
